title: Ephemera mounting techniques
subtitle: Attaching tickets, labels, postcards, and found papers to scrapbook pages without damage
excerpt: Ephemera (tickets, stamps, labels, postcards, wrappers, and other found papers) gives scrapbook pages authenticity and texture. The challenge is mounting fragile or irregular items without flattening them or using adhesives that will damage them over time. This tutorial covers flat mounting, semi-detached pockets, envelope mounts, and sewn attachment.
sourceNotes: The practice of mounting paper ephemera is documented in Victorian scrapbook and commonplace book traditions. The attachment methods described here are a synthesis of archival and craft mounting techniques.

glossaryTerms.definition: Printed or paper items not originally intended for long-term preservation: tickets, receipts, labels, wrappers, postcards, and similar found or collected papers. In scrapbooking, ephemera adds documentary and tactile interest to a layout.
A mounting method in which an item is secured along one or two edges only, leaving part of it free to lift. Allows items to be turned over or unfolded without removing them from the page.
An adhesive that is acid-free and pH-neutral, so it does not cause yellowing or degradation of paper over time. Preferred for attaching photographs and important documents.

aliases: attaching tickets and labels to pages

content.text: The key question with any piece of
is whether to mount it flat or leave it partially free. Flat mounting is fastest and most stable;
adds dimension and lets the viewer interact with the item. Both methods benefit from
on items you want to preserve. For disposable or reprinted ephemera, any clean glue stick is adequate.

items.name: Acid-free PVA or glue stick (for archival-quality mounting)
Washi tape (for semi-detached mounts and pocket hinges)
Small envelopes or glassine bags (for pocket mounting small items)
Bone folder (for pressing flat after gluing)
Craft knife and ruler (for cutting mounting corners and pockets)

content.text: Apply a thin even layer of adhesive to the back of the item. Cover the full back surface for small items; apply around the edges only for items larger than postcard size (central gluing can cause rippling).
Press the item firmly to the page, working from the centre outward. Place a clean sheet of paper over the item and apply pressure with the bone folder for 30 seconds.
Apply glue or washi tape to the top edge of the item only. Press this top edge to the page. The bottom, sides, and back of the item remain free.
The item can be lifted, turned over, or slid aside. This method works well for items with content on both sides (folded maps, multi-page pamphlets, folded letters).

Glue a small envelope, glassine bag, or folded paper pocket to the page. Slide the ephemera item inside. The item is fully protected and removable. Label the outside of the pocket with a caption or date so the contents are identifiable when the pocket is closed. This method suits very fragile or three-dimensional items.

Cut small right-angle triangles from black or white card and fold them into corner pockets. Glue the pockets to the page at the four corners of the item's position, leaving the triangular pocket opening facing inward. Slide the item's corners into the pockets. The item is held securely but can be removed without any adhesive touching it.
